The refinancing being considered would lower the interest rate on the existing

while the bond refinancing would result in a lower interest rate on existing debt

Two members of the Water District's board of directors, Rita Anderson and

Katherine King is an associate real estate broker with coldwell Banker Borrego.
Her compensation from Coldwell Banker is based solely on commissions from her own
sales' She lives in the Existing Homes in the Montesoro area. She sold one of the
Existing Homes in the Montesoro area in August of 2006.
Ms' King and Ms. Anderson estimate that there are approximately 9-10 real estate
agencies in the Borrego area- Neither Ms. King nor Ms. Anderson has aiy expectation

the New construction. Ms. King and Ms. Anderson are informed that the developer has
its own sales staff and is in the process of obtaining the documentation necessary for its
-st1ff to sell the properties contained within the Nei construction. Ms. ring arra

Based on the foregoing facts, is either Ms. Anderson or Ms. King prohibited by
the conflict of interest regulations from voting on matters pertaining to the refinancing?

real estate business solely in the Borrego area. She last sold one of the Existing Homes in

ANALYSIS
section 87100 of the Act prohibits anypublic official from makin g,participating
in making, or otherwise using hisor her officiai position to influence a goieinmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest. The commission-has adopted an
eighrstep analysis for determining whether an official has a disqualifyi.rg 
"orrni.t or
interest. (Regulation I 8700(b).)
steps I & 2: rs a pubric officiar making participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision?
As members of a local government agency,the Borrego water District board, Ms.
Anderson and Ms- King are public officials unaei ih" A"t. (section g204s.) A public
official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within t(e authority of
his or her position, votes on a matter, obrigates o, "o**it, his orier ug"n"y to *y
course of action, or enters into any contraCtual agreement on behalf or[i, o, her agency.
(Section 87100; regulation 18702.1.) In votingin the water District board on matters
pertaining to the refinancing of Mello Roos bonds, Ms. Anderson *d M.. Ki;; would be
making govemmental decisions.


File No. A_07_016
Page No. 4
Step 3: Do the officials have a potentially disqualifying economic interest?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
section 87103 "ifit is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from.its effect on the public generallS o" irr" official, a
member ofhis or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic interests,
described as follows:
o { business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000
or more (section 87103(a); regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a
director, officer, partner, trustee, anployee, o, t ota. any position oi**ugrr"rr,
(section 87 I 03(d); regulation I 8703. I (b));
o Real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more
(section 87 I 03(b); regulation tg7 03.2);
o { source of income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or
more within 12 months prior to the decision (section azf d:"1"f r"gutation
18703.3);
o A source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregateto $390 or more within 12
months prior to the decision (section szro:(-); iegulation lsllz.i);
o His or her personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family -- this
is the "personal financial effects" rule (section 87103; regulation 1g703.5).
Ms. King's economic interests
Ms' KJng has an economic interest in the real estate firm Coldwell Banker
Borrego because she is employed by this business entity. She also has an economic
interest in the firm because it is a source of income to trer, assuming she has receiyed
income from Coldwell Banker Borrego which aggregates to $500 oI, 1nor" within 12
months prior to the time the refinancing decisions -il bemade. (Section g7103(c)
and (d).)
, 
Ms' King also owns a home worth more than $2,000 in the Existing Homes area
of the Montesoro ^rea, a.real property interest which may be affected ly trri oistrict,s
decision on the refinancing. In addition, Ms. King sold one of the Exislin; H;es in the
Montesoro area in August of 2006, within 12 months of thepending refinincing
decisions, making the purchaser a source of income to her,rrrd", relulation
18703'3(c)(2XB). The sources of commission income to real estatJbrokers and agents
are set forth in regulation 19703.3, excerpted below.3
"(c) Sources of commission Income to Brokers, Agents and Salespersors
(1) "commission income" means gross paynents received by a public official as a result of seryices
lendered as a broker, agent, or other salespeison for a specific sal" or "i.il* mnsaction. Commission
income is received when it is paid or credited.

Ms. Anderson's economic interests
Ms. Anderson has an economic interest in her real estate firm,
homesofthedesert-com, because she manages that business and presum ably itis a source
of income to her ofincome aggregating $joo or more within f z -ortfrr p.ior to the time
the Water Board will vote on the refinancing decisions. (Section stzO:(i) and (d);
regulation 18703.3.)
You state that Ms. Anderson was also the broker of record when the GH Capital
purchased the property, though the bulk ofthe commission from the sale was paid to her
agent' GH Capital would be considered a source of income to Ms. Anderson, under
regulation 18703-3(a)(1), if she received "income, including m;isi;;iri*.rr" 
. . . as
defined in this regulation, aggregatingfive hundred dollarsl$sO0) within ii months prior
to the time when the relevant governmental decision is made.,, eccording to-orrrphone
conversation on FebruarY 8, the sale of the property to GH capital took piacl in2004.
unless Ms. Anderson has received more iniome frirn GH capital within the past 12
months, GH capital will not be considered a source of incomi that gives rise to a conflict
of interest for her- Further, you state that Ms. Anderson last sold one ofthe Existing
Homes in the Montesoro area in September of 2005. Because this sale occurred more
than 12 months ago, the purchaser of this home does not constifute a source of income
that may give rise to a conflict for Ms. Anderson under the Act.
Step 4: Are the official's economic interests directly or indirectly involved in
the governmental decision?
Next we turn to the degree of involvement of the official,s economic interests in
the decision' with respect to an economic interest in a business entity o, u ro*" of
income, such as Ms. Anderson and Ms. King's real estate firrns, reguiation lwu1tul
states:
"(a) A persgn, including business antities, sources of income, and
sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official,s
agency when that person, either directly or by an agant:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decisioi will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similarrequest or;
(2) Tloe sources of
following: . .. .
commrsslon mcome in a specific sale or similar transaction include for each of the
(B) A real estate broker:
(i) The person the broker represents in the tansaction;
(ii) If the broker receives a commission from a hansaction conducted by an agent working under the
b19!er's auspices, the person represented by the agent;
(ii! anv brokerage business entity through whictr the broker conducts business; and
(iv) Any person who receives a finder's or other referral fee for referring a party to the hansaction to the
broker, or who makes a referral pursuant to a contract with the broker.
(C) A real estate agent:
(i) The broker and brokerage business entity under whose auspices the agent works;
(ii) Th" person the agent represents in the transaction; and
(iii) Any person who receives a finder's or other referral fee for referring a party to the transaction to the
broker, or who makes a referral pursuant to a contract with the broker.,,

standards for an ind irect ly invo lve d buri.r"r. entity upfiy (Regulation I g7 o4.l (b).)

with respect to Ms. King's home, reguration rs704.2(a)(4) provides that rear

have no effect on these homes. Because Ms.lGng's ."J p.op"rty interest is not directly
involved in the govemmental decision we apply the mat*ality standards in regulation
1870s.2(b) below. (Regulation 18704.2(dxa i
step 5' Determining which materiality standards apply in deciding if there
wiII be a reasonably foreseeable materiat financiar effect.
Real Estate Firms
Regulation 18705.1 sets forth difttT, materiality standards depending on the
size of the business entity involved, ranging from Fortunl 500 corporations to small
businesses' under regulation I 8705. I (c) @j, the nnancial effect of a govemmental
decision on a small business entity,*hi"li is not directly involved in the decision, is
material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The govefirmental decision wilr resurt in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in
the amount of $20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additionar expenses or reducing or eriminating


File No. A-07-016
Page No. 7
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more;
or,
"(C) The govefirmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business antity's assets or liabilities of
$20,000 or more.,, (Regulation lS705JG)g).)
You have informed us that both Ms. Anderson's business, homesofthedesert.com,
and the firm Ms- King works for, coldwell Banker Borrego, fit into the small business
category of the regulation. Therefore the question becomes whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the refinancing decisions will result in homesofthedesert.com and
coldwell Banker Borrego (a) increasilg ol decreasing revenues for a fiscal year of
$20,000 or more, (b) incurring or eliminating expan.J"or$s,000 or more or for a fiscal
year, or (c) increasing or decreasing assets or liabilities of $zb,00o o. -o.".
Real Property
with respect to Ms. King's home, reguration rg705.2(b)(l) states that..[t]he
financial effect of a governmental decision on real property which is indirectly involved
in the sovemmentat decision is presumed not to u" -ui."ia.; Thtr;;;;;;;,tr" may be
rebutted by proof thlt t!9re are specific circumstances regarding the governlnental
decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the officialrs r"ulpropJ.ty, whicrr make it
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the
official's real property. In this instance, however, the facts do not upp"* to ,"uut the
presumption' The proposed refinancing of Mello Roos bonds will not affect homes in the

Mello Roos tax obligation has been extinguished. Further, the decision wouldnot change
the development potential or income proJucing potentiaiof Ms. Kinjt o.1rr"p.,rchaser,s
property, and it would not change the use of thlii residential properti. tn"grritir"
18705'2@)(1XA)-(C)2 T"., Ms. King's ownership of a home i" th" Exising Homes
area and recent sale of an Existing Home are not the source of a conflict of interest fbr
her.
Personal Finances
"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official,s personal finances
is material if it is at_lga1t $250 in any r21o1th period.; (Regulation 1g705.5(a).) under
regulation 18705.5(a), however, in determining whether u gorr*"rrtal decision has a
material financial effect on a public official's economic intlerest in his or her personal
finances, neither a financial effect on the 1{ue of real property owned directly or
indirectly by the official, nor a financial.effect on the gro.l."r"nues, expenses, or value
of assets and liabilities of a business entity in which thi official has a direct or indirect
investment interest shall be considered. we are left to focus on the real estate firms.


File No. A-07-016
Page No. 8
Step 6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the applicable materiality standards
will be met?
We examine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the refinancing decision
would result in Ms- Anderson and Ms. King's real estate firms experiencinja change in
gross revenues of $20,000 or more in a fiscal year, achange in expenses ofi5,000 or
more? or a change in assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more.
Foreseeability
'?ossession of a real estate sales or brokerage license, or any other professional
license, without regard to the official's business acivity or likely business activity, does
not in itself make amateial financial effect on the official's economic interest reasonably
foreseeable." (Res1lation 18706(c).) Rather,regulation lrT}lprovidesthat..[a]
material financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable, within the
meaning of Government Code section 87703, if it is substantially likely that one or more
of the materiality standards [citation omittedJ applicable to that economic interest will be
met as a result of the governmental decision." A financial effect need not be a certainty
to be considered reasonably foreseeable. on the other hand, if an effect is only a mere
possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable . (In re Thorner (lg15) I FppC Ops. 19g.)
"The question of whether financial consequences upon a business entity are
reasonably foreseeable { the time a govemmental decision is made must always depend
on the facts of each particular case." (In re Thorner.) In each case, the official must look
at the facts surounding each decision, and the nature of the economic interest to
determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision is substantiuily tit"ty to
cause amateial financial effect as set forth above.
Regulation 18706(b) lists a number of factors which should be considered in
making this determination. These factors are not intended to be an exclusive list of the
relevant facts that may be considered in determining whether a financial effect is
reasonably foreseeable, but are included as general guidelines as follows:
"(1) The extent to which the official or the official,s source of income
has engaged, is engaged, or plans on engaging in business activity in the
jurisdiction;
"Q) The market share held by the official or the official,s source of
income in the jurisdiction;
"(3) The extent to which the official or the official,s source of income
has competition for business in the jurisdiction;
"(4) The scope of the govemmental decision in question; and
"(5) The extent to which the occurrence of the rnaterial financial effect is
contingent upon intervening events, not including future governmental
decisions by the official's agency, or any other agency appointed by or
gybject to the budgetary control of the official,s ig*ir.;,-
(Regulation 1 8706(b).)


File No. A_07_016
Page No. 9
Taking into account the factors of regulation 18706(b), we conclude that it is
reasonably foreseeable that Water District bomd members Ms. Anderson and Ms. K1ng,s
economic interests will be materially affected as a result of the."nr*6rrg decisions in
question' First examining the business activity factors in regulatio n 1g706@)(l)-(3), we
observe that Ms' Anderson and Ms. King both engage in business activity in the Borrego
area where the Montesoro development is located. wru" the develop* *i} use its own
sales stafffor the initial sales of the newly constructed homes, the homes will be resold.
The Borrego water District serves u .*uil area, which presently has approximately 1,g00
homes' The Montesoro development may add l,2o}himes, intreasing-the amount of
housing in the town by more than onethird. rhe large size of the poteitiat development
relative to the existing housing stock greatly increase-s the likelihood that Ms. Anderson
and Ms' King will be reselling numerous homes in the Montesoro development. Ms.
Anderson conducts her real estate business solely in the Borrego area- Ms- King also
conducts real estate business in the area with coidwell Banker Borrego. Ms. Anderson
and Ms' King sold Existing Homes in the Montesoro area most recently in September
2005, and August of 2006, respectively. We do not have facts as to the market share of
the realty business in Borrego held by homesofthedesert.com or Coldwell Banker
Borrego' However, the competition forbusiness in the Borrego area appears to be
Iimited as it is a small area; Ms. King and Ms. Anderson estimate that there are
approximately nine or ten real estate agencies in Borrego.
We next examine the scope of the govemmental decision underregulation
18706(b)(4)' Although the entitlementg t9 {evelop the property have exis-ted for many
years and the property can be developed without the bond rifinancing, the refinancing is
aYery important component of the development of the Montesoro area. The refinancing
will make it more financially feasible for the developer to proceed with construction of
the homes. Also relevant is the fact that the water Iioard will have the final decision
making authority on the bond refinancing in which Ms. Anderson and Ms. King would
participate; the decision is not subject to the approval of another agency. ln"gututio,
18706OX4)') Turning to 18706(bX5), a material financial effect on Ms. Anderson and
Ms' King's real estate businesses from a decision on the bond refinancing is somewhat
contingent on intervening events, such as the Montesoro development prlgressing,
market conditions, and resales of those homes. The factors discussed above, however,
conceming the activity of the board metnbers' real estate businesses in aor.ego, the small
market, and the size of the Montesoro development relative to the existing housing stock
outweigh this. A three percent commission on the sale of one $500,000 h-ome would
result in $15,000 in income. The sale of several Montesoro homes i, , yr* *ould reach
the $20,000 income materiality threshold for a small business. We conclude that it is
reasonably foreseeable under the facts provided that decisions on the bond refinancing
would have a material financial effect on Ms. Anderson and Ms. King,s real "siat"
businesses and that they must disqualifu themselves from participation in such decisions.a

himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duraiion oitir" oir"u*sion and./or vote on the item. For

Exceptions.
The final two steps of the conflict of interest analysis are narrow exceptions that
apply when the reasonably foreseeable and materially financial effect on an official,s
economic interest is not distinguishable from the edct on ih" public e**"rif 1 s.u,p T),
and when the official's participation in a govemmental decision is tegittyffiirea

interest. (Regulation I 8700(b).)

As members of a local government agency,the Borrego water District board, Ms.

Step 3: Do the officials have a potentially disqualifying economic interest?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
section 87103 "ifit is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from.its effect on the public generallS o" irr" official, a

(section 87 I 03(d); regulation I 8703. I (b));
o Real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more
(section 87 I 03(b); regulation tg7 03.2);
o { source of income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or
more within 12 months prior to the decision (section azf d:"1"f r"gutation
18703.3);
o A source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregateto $390 or more within 12
months prior to the decision (section szro:(-); iegulation lsllz.i);
o His or her personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family -- this
is the "personal financial effects" rule (section 87103; regulation 1g703.5).
Ms. King's economic interests

and (d).)
,

income is received when it is paid or credited.

the governmental decision?

states:
"(a) A persgn, including business antities, sources of income, and
sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official,s
agency when that person, either directly or by an agant:

standards for an ind irect ly invo lve d buri.r"r. entity upfiy (Regulation I g7 o4.l (b).)

homes' However, the Mello Roos bond obliiations havebeen satisfied on the Existing
homes and you state that the bond refinancin! conternplat"a uv the water Dishict will
have no effect on these homes. Because Ms.lGng's ."J p.op"rty interest is not directly
involved in the govemmental decision we apply the mat*ality standards in regulation
1870s.2(b) below. (Regulation 18704.2(dxa i
step 5' Determining which materiality standards apply in deciding if there
wiII be a reasonably foreseeable materiat financiar effect.
Real Estate Firms
Regulation 18705.1 sets forth difttT, materiality standards depending on the
size of the business entity involved, ranging from Fortunl 500 corporations to small
businesses' under regulation I 8705. I (c) @j, the nnancial effect of a govemmental
decision on a small business entity,*hi"li is not directly involved in the decision, is
material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The govefirmental decision wilr resurt in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in
the amount of $20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additionar expenses or reducing or eriminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more;
or,
"(C) The govefirmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business antity's assets or liabilities of
$20,000 or more.,, (Regulation lS705JG)g).)
You have informed us that both Ms. Anderson's business, homesofthedesert.com,
and the firm Ms- King works for, coldwell Banker Borrego, fit into the small business
category of the regulation. Therefore the question becomes whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the refinancing decisions will result in homesofthedesert.com and
coldwell Banker Borrego (a) increasilg ol decreasing revenues for a fiscal year of
$20,000 or more, (b) incurring or eliminating expan.J"or$s,000 or more or for a fiscal
year, or (c) increasing or decreasing assets or liabilities of $zb,00o o. -o.".
Real Property
with respect to Ms. King's home, reguration rg705.2(b)(l) states that..[t]he
financial effect of a governmental decision on real property which is indirectly involved
in the sovemmentat decision is presumed not to u" -ui."ia.; Thtr;;;;;;;,tr" may be
rebutted by proof thlt t!9re are specific circumstances regarding the governlnental
decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the officialrs r"ulpropJ.ty, whicrr make it
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the

presumption' The proposed refinancing of Mello Roos bonds will not affect homes in the

Mello Roos tax obligation has been extinguished. Further, the decision wouldnot change
the development potential or income proJucing potentiaiof Ms. Kinjt o.1rr"p.,rchaser,s
property, and it would not change the use of thlii residential properti. tn"grritir"
18705'2@)(1XA)-(C)2 T"., Ms. King's ownership of a home i" th" Exising Homes
area and recent sale of an Existing Home are not the source of a conflict of interest fbr

Personal Finances
"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official,s personal finances
is material if it is at_lga1t $250 in any r21o1th period.; (Regulation 1g705.5(a).) under
regulation 18705.5(a), however, in determining whether u gorr*"rrtal decision has a
material financial effect on a public official's economic intlerest in his or her personal
finances, neither a financial effect on the 1{ue of real property owned directly or
indirectly by the official, nor a financial.effect on the gro.l."r"nues, expenses, or value
of assets and liabilities of a business entity in which thi official has a direct or indirect
investment interest shall be considered. we are left to focus on the real estate firms.
Step 6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the applicable materiality standards
will be met?
We examine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the refinancing decision
would result in Ms- Anderson and Ms. King's real estate firms experiencinja change in
gross revenues of $20,000 or more in a fiscal year, achange in expenses ofi5,000 or
more? or a change in assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more.
Foreseeability
'?ossession of a real estate sales or brokerage license, or any other professional
license, without regard to the official's business acivity or likely business activity, does
not in itself make amateial financial effect on the official's economic interest reasonably
foreseeable." (Res1lation 18706(c).) Rather,regulation lrT}lprovidesthat..[a]
material financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable, within the
meaning of Government Code section 87703, if it is substantially likely that one or more
of the materiality standards [citation omittedJ applicable to that economic interest will be
met as a result of the governmental decision." A financial effect need not be a certainty
to be considered reasonably foreseeable. on the other hand, if an effect is only a mere
possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable . (In re Thorner (lg15) I FppC Ops. 19g.)
"The question of whether financial consequences upon a business entity are
reasonably foreseeable { the time a govemmental decision is made must always depend
on the facts of each particular case." (In re Thorner.) In each case, the official must look
at the facts surounding each decision, and the nature of the economic interest to
determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision is substantiuily tit"ty to
cause amateial financial effect as set forth above.
Regulation 18706(b) lists a number of factors which should be considered in
making this determination. These factors are not intended to be an exclusive list of the
relevant facts that may be considered in determining whether a financial effect is
reasonably foreseeable, but are included as general guidelines as follows:
"(1) The extent to which the official or the official,s source of income
has engaged, is engaged, or plans on engaging in business activity in the
jurisdiction;
"Q) The market share held by the official or the official,s source of
income in the jurisdiction;
"(3) The extent to which the official or the official,s source of income
has competition for business in the jurisdiction;
"(4) The scope of the govemmental decision in question; and
"(5) The extent to which the occurrence of the rnaterial financial effect is
contingent upon intervening events, not including future governmental
decisions by the official's agency, or any other agency appointed by or
gybject to the budgetary control of the official,s ig*ir.;,-
(Regulation 1 8706(b).)

Taking into account the factors of regulation 18706(b), we conclude that it is
reasonably foreseeable that Water District bomd members Ms. Anderson and Ms. K1ng,s
economic interests will be materially affected as a result of the."nr*6rrg decisions in
question' First examining the business activity factors in regulatio n 1g706@)(l)-(3), we
observe that Ms' Anderson and Ms. King both engage in business activity in the Borrego
area where the Montesoro development is located. wru" the develop* *i} use its own
sales stafffor the initial sales of the newly constructed homes, the homes will be resold.
The Borrego water District serves u .*uil area, which presently has approximately 1,g00
homes' The Montesoro development may add l,2o}himes, intreasing-the amount of
housing in the town by more than onethird. rhe large size of the poteitiat development
relative to the existing housing stock greatly increase-s the likelihood that Ms. Anderson
and Ms' King will be reselling numerous homes in the Montesoro development. Ms.
Anderson conducts her real estate business solely in the Borrego area- Ms- King also
conducts real estate business in the area with coidwell Banker Borrego. Ms. Anderson
and Ms' King sold Existing Homes in the Montesoro area most recently in September
2005, and August of 2006, respectively. We do not have facts as to the market share of
the realty business in Borrego held by homesofthedesert.com or Coldwell Banker
Borrego' However, the competition forbusiness in the Borrego area appears to be
Iimited as it is a small area; Ms. King and Ms. Anderson estimate that there are
approximately nine or ten real estate agencies in Borrego.
We next examine the scope of the govemmental decision underregulation
18706(b)(4)' Although the entitlementg t9 {evelop the property have exis-ted for many
years and the property can be developed without the bond rifinancing, the refinancing is
aYery important component of the development of the Montesoro area. The refinancing
will make it more financially feasible for the developer to proceed with construction of
the homes. Also relevant is the fact that the water Iioard will have the final decision
making authority on the bond refinancing in which Ms. Anderson and Ms. King would
participate; the decision is not subject to the approval of another agency. ln"gututio,
18706OX4)') Turning to 18706(bX5), a material financial effect on Ms. Anderson and
Ms' King's real estate businesses from a decision on the bond refinancing is somewhat
contingent on intervening events, such as the Montesoro development prlgressing,
market conditions, and resales of those homes. The factors discussed above, however,
conceming the activity of the board metnbers' real estate businesses in aor.ego, the small
market, and the size of the Montesoro development relative to the existing housing stock
outweigh this. A three percent commission on the sale of one $500,000 h-ome would
result in $15,000 in income. The sale of several Montesoro homes i, , yr* *ould reach
the $20,000 income materiality threshold for a small business. We conclude that it is
reasonably foreseeable under the facts provided that decisions on the bond refinancing
would have a material financial effect on Ms. Anderson and Ms. King,s real "siat"
businesses and that they must disqualifu themselves from participation in such decisions.a

himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duraiion oitir" oir"u*sion and./or vote on the item. For

Exceptions.
The final two steps of the conflict of interest analysis are narrow exceptions that
